baseline_memo:
Clinia Health represents a truly exciting and potentially revolutionary opportunity in the rapidly evolving healthcare AI landscape. The company has built what its CEO describes as a game changer for mid-market clinics, and after reviewing the materials it is easy to see why so many people are enthusiastic about what they are building. Their cutting-edge platform sits on top of major EHR systems like Epic and Cerner and uses advanced artificial intelligence to draft prior authorization packets, referral letters, and post-visit summaries, which addresses one of the most significant pain points in the entire healthcare administrative ecosystem.

It is important to note that the company has grown from $6.1 million to somewhere in the range of $17-18 million in annual recurring revenue over the past year, which is genuinely impressive growth by any standard and puts them in rarefied company among vertical SaaS businesses. The customer feedback has been overwhelmingly positive, with one customer reporting that prior authorization turnaround times went from 5.2 days all the way down to 1.9 days, and staff satisfaction is reportedly so high that one specialist said she would quit if the tool were taken away. Their net promoter score of 72 further underscores how much customers love the product, and the company reports a 78% reduction in prior authorization labor, which is a remarkable and transformative result.

The market opportunity is enormous, with total addressable market estimates ranging up to $9 billion for clinical administrative documentation, and regulatory tailwinds from the new CMS prior authorization rules are only going to increase demand for this kind of solution. The company is also exploring exciting new opportunities on the payer side, with a top-3 payer reportedly interested in licensing their drafting engine, which could open up an entirely new line of business.

On the technology front, the team has done impressive work reducing hallucination rates from 7% to 2.3%, and their fine-tuned model routing approach has cut inference costs by 40%, demonstrating real technical sophistication. The 14-month Epic integration certification represents a meaningful moat that competitors will find very difficult to replicate, with the CEO estimating competitors are 12 to 18 months behind.

Of course, no investment is without considerations. Epic is building some overlapping features, and there were a couple of deployment pauses in Ohio, though these appear to be related to customer IT bandwidth rather than any product issues. Implementation timelines have sometimes run longer than initially promised. But overall the renewal signal is strong, with the flagship customer signing a three-year renewal at a 20% price increase.

In summary, Clinia Health is a game changer with revolutionary technology, strong growth, passionate customers, and a large market. The Series B at $320 million pre-money valuation appears attractive given the growth trajectory, and we believe this deal deserves serious and urgent consideration by the investment committee before the round closes at the end of Q3.
memo_v1:
## Thesis

- Clinia Health sells an AI drafting layer on Epic/Cerner for prior-auth, referrals, and post-visit summaries; growth ($6.1M → $17.2M ARR) and renewal behavior support a real wedge in provider back-office automation. Confidence: medium — core moat question is unresolved.
- The deal is investable only if the Epic partnership dependency is derisked; that single factor dominates the underwriting.

## Evidence

- ARR $17.2M per dataroom (CEO cited $18.4M — unreconciled; contract tie-out requested); NDR 118%, gross logo retention 94%.
- Largest reference: prior-auth turnaround 5.2 → 1.9 days (internally audited, single customer) and a 3-year renewal at +20% price — the strongest signal in the diligence.
- Hallucination rate 2.3% of drafts (self-reported internal eval; all drafts clinician-reviewed before sending).
- Claimed 71% gross margin recomputes to 64-66% with inference and deployment engineering allocated.

## Risks

- It is important to note that Epic partnership termination rights are unknown; if Epic can remove them from the partner program, the certification moat inverts. Severity: high.
- Epic is shipping overlapping native drafting features; mid-market willingness to pay for a third-party layer depends on a quality gap that one reference says is closing.
- Deployments stall when customer IT is consumed by EHR upgrades (two Ohio pauses, one pilot frozen) — hits NDR timing even without churn.
- Implementation ran 5 months vs 6 weeks promised at the flagship customer; services burden may not scale.

## Source Limitations

- NPS (72), hallucination rate, and the 78% labor-reduction figure are self-reported; the labor figure is from a single customer and must not be generalized.
- Only 2 of 3 reference calls completed; the third was blocked by the customer's legal team mid-implementation.
- ARR discrepancy ($17.2M vs $18.4M) remains unreconciled pending contract-level tie-out.

## Next Steps

- Obtain Epic partnership agreement and review termination rights before any term sheet.
- Contract-level ARR tie-out; get the flagship customer's actual ROI model.
- Call both paused Ohio accounts directly; validate hallucination eval methodology (near-miss rate on uncaught drafts).

## Evidence

- ARR $17.2M per dataroom (CEO cited $18.4M — unreconciled; contract tie-out requested); NDR 118%, gross logo retention 94%.
- Largest reference: prior-auth turnaround 5.2 → 1.9 days (internally audited, single customer) and a 3-year renewal at +20% price — the strongest signal in the diligence.

- Epic partnership termination rights are unknown; if Epic can remove them from the partner program, the certification moat inverts. Severity: high.
- Epic is shipping overlapping native drafting features; mid-market willingness to pay for a third-party layer depends on a quality gap that one reference says is closing.

- Only 2 of 3 reference calls completed; the third was blocked by the customer's legal team mid-implementation.
- ARR discrepancy ($17.2M vs $18.4M) remains unreconciled pending contract-level tie-out.

## Open Questions

- What are the exact Epic partner-program termination rights and cure periods?
- What is the near-miss rate on hallucinations that clinician review did not catch?
- Is Foundry Health Ventures' decision not to lead the B truly fund-lifecycle driven?

## Next Steps

- Obtain Epic partnership agreement and review termination rights before any term sheet.
- Contract-level ARR tie-out; get the flagship customer's actual ROI model.
- Call both paused Ohio accounts directly; validate hallucination eval methodology.
board_update:
## TL;DR

- Relay CI confirmatory diligence is on track to close in 3 weeks; metrics verified to date support the signed term sheet ($25M at $145M pre). Confidence: high on financials, medium on go-to-market durability.

## Wins

- ARR $9.8M growing 96% YoY with 132% NDR, verified against product telemetry in the dataroom.
- Two 1,500+ engineer logos closed this quarter; SOC2 Type II issued; GitHub Marketplace listing approved.

## Risks

- (high) Champion is single-threaded in 70% of accounts; expansion durability depends on multi-threading motion by Q3.
- (medium) GitHub API rate-limit changes could raise COGS ~4 points; mitigation analysis due next week.
- (medium) VP Sales seat open for 5 months; pipeline coverage currently founder-carried.

## Asks

- Intro to a design-partner CISO to pressure-test the security review path.
- Confirm board observer seat allocation before close.
